## PAGE 00134: (2026x Refresh1) How to approve requirements

- page_id: `304002356`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/304002356/2026x+Refresh1+How+to+approve+requirements
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2026-04-29T10:51:53.117+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) User Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud > (2026x Refresh1) Editing and contextualizing > (2026x Refresh1) Model editing use cases
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

One of the ways you can benefit from editing your model in a Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document is reviewing and approving Requirements. The following workflow will give you instructions how to do it:

1. . You will need custom Requirements to be able to change their Status (e.g., to Approved).
2. Use the Profile with the custom Requirement subtype in the model you intend to publish for review .
3. In the model, create and define Requirements of the custom Requirement subtype you have created. Converting default requirementsIf you already have default requirements defined in your model, you can convert them to the custom Requirement subtype by [refactoring](https://docs.nomagic.com/MT/?contextKey=refactoring&version=2026x Refresh1&variant=default).
4. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Publishing documents' space=''] (or the part of the model containing Requirements) to Cameo Collaborator for review and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Sharing document links' space=''] with reviewers.
5. .

##### Creating a custom Requirement subtype

If you want to change Requirement status, you need to create a custom Requirement stereotype with the Status tag, as described below.

To create a custom Requirement subtype

1. Create a new SysML v1 project .
2. In the project, Create a Profile element and name it Custom Stereotypes .
3. In the Custom Stereotypes Profile, create a Profile Diagram .
4. In the Profile Diagram, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stereotype' space='MED'] and name it Custom Requirement .
5. In the Model Browser, find the Requirement [Class] element (it should be located in the SysML Profile) and drag it to the Profile Diagram.
6. Create a Generalization relationship from the Custom Requirement to the Requirement [Class] .
7. In the same Profile Diagram, create an Enumeration instance, as displayed below, and name it Status . [ATTACHMENT filename='creating_enumeration_instance.png']
8. Create three Enumeration Literals for the Status Enumeration instance, as displayed below, and name them In Progress , Approved , and Disapproved . [ATTACHMENT filename='creating_enumaration_literal.png']
9. Drag the Status Enumeration instance shape to the Custom Requirement Stereotype shape. Your Profile Diagram should look like the one shown below. [ATTACHMENT filename='profile_diagram.png']

Now that you have created a profile with a custom Requirement subtype, you can use it in the model where you want to create custom Requirements.

##### Approving Requirements

To approve Requirements

1. Open the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document with the Requirements you want to review. See the example of a Requirements table below. [ATTACHMENT filename='requirements_table.png']
2. Review the requirements and do one or several of the following actions:
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Editing element values' space='']Change the **Status** value of the Requirements that do not need any changes to **Approved**. Empty values cannot be editedYou can change only existing Status values. Cameo Collaborator does not allow to enter property values that have not been specified. So make sure that certain Status values (e.g., In Progress) are defined before [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Publishing documents' space=''].
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Working with comments in Cameo Collaborator' space=''] for items that need to be discussed.
3. Inform the author that you have finished reviewing the document.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304002143 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00135: (2026x Refresh1) How to specify Interfaces

- page_id: `304002143`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/304002143/2026x+Refresh1+How+to+specify+Interfaces
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T10:47:19.378+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) User Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud > (2026x Refresh1) Editing and contextualizing > (2026x Refresh1) Model editing use cases
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

If you are responsible for for a set of interfaces or an interface library, you can enter interface values (e.g. allowed voltages) via a Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document.

To specify interface values

1. Open the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document with the Interfaces you want to specify. See the sample table displaying the allowed voltages of Interfaces below. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Do one or several of the following actions:
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Editing element values' space=''] (e.g., minimum and maximum voltages). Empty values cannot be editedYou can change only the existing values of Interfaces. Cameo Collaborator does not allow to enter property values that have not been specified. So make sure that certain Interface values are defined before [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Publishing documents' space=''].
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Working with comments in Cameo Collaborator' space=''] for values that need to be discussed.
3. Inform the author that you have finished updating the model.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

See the sample table displaying the allowed voltages of Interfaces below.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="specifying_interfaces.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) How to specify Interfaces" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Do one or several of the following actions:</span><br /><ul><li><p><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Editing element values" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Specify the values of Interfaces]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> (e.g., minimum and maximum voltages).</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a68b17de-b140-4c57-8055-083a564eb5f0"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Empty values cannot be edited</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can change only the existing values of Interfaces. Cameo Collaborator does not allow to enter property values that have not been specified. So make sure that certain Interface values are defined before <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Publishing documents" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[publishing a model for editing]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Working with comments in Cameo Collaborator" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Create textual or graphical comments]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for values that need to be discussed.</span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><br /></span></li></ul></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Inform the author that you have finished updating the model.</span></li></ol><p><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304003316 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00136: (2026x Refresh1) Identifying suspicious usages

- page_id: `304003316`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/304003316/2026x+Refresh1+Identifying+suspicious+usages
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T10:49:22.146+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) User Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Resource Usage Map
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In the Resource Usage Map application, you can detect three types of suspicious usages: inconsistent version usages, cyclic usages, and unused resources. In addition, you can filter and investigate suspicious usages by using the Filters pane. The sections below explain how to do that.

##### Inconsistent version usages

The inconsistent version usages filter allows you to see which resources use different versions of the same resource directly and through other used projects. You can investigate every inconsistent version usage displayed in your resource usage map as described below.

To investigate an inconsistent version usage

1. In the Filters pane of a resource usage map, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] next to the inconsistent version usages filter to see the list of all the inconsistent version usages in the map.
2. Select the inconsistent version usage you want to investigate. A detailed map of that usage is created in the content pane.
3. Click the **Details** button on the top right corner of the inconsistent version usage map, to see full information of the selected usage as illustrated in the following figure. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As you can see in the example map below, *Car Structure library* directly uses version 20.0 of *MD_customization_for_SysML* but other resources used by *Car Structure library* use version 19.0 v9 of *MD_customization_for_SysML* (indirect usage).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of an inconsistent version usage map.

##### Cyclic usages

The cyclic usages filter allows you to see which resourcesuse themselves through other used projects and form a usage loop. You can investigate every cyclic usage displayed in your resource usage map as described below.

To investigate a cyclic usage

1. In the Filters pane of a resource usage map, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] next to the cyclic usages filter to see the list of all the cyclic usages in the map.
2. Select the cyclic usage you want to investigate. A detailed map of that usage is created in the content pane.
3. Click the **Details** button on the top right corner of the cyclic usage map, to see full information of the selected usage as illustrated in the following figure. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As you can see in the example map below, *MD_customization_for_SysML* uses itself by using *ISO-80000* which uses *QUDV_SysML 1.4* and *QUDV_SysML 1.4* uses *MD_customization_for_SysML* thus forming a usage loop.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of a cyclic usage map.

##### Unused resources

The unused resources filter allows you to see which resources are not used by other resources stored in the Teamwork Cloud repository. Thisfilter can only be displayed in a map created for all the resources in the repository.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

You can investigate every cyclic usage displayed in your resource usage map as described below.</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To investigate a cyclic usage</span></p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the Filters pane of a resource usage map, click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="expand.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Identifying suspicious usages" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> next to the cyclic usages filter to see the list of all the cyclic usages in the map.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Select the cyclic usage you want to investigate. A detailed map of that usage is created in the content pane.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Click the <strong>Details</strong> button on the top right corner of the cyclic usage map, to see full information of the selected usage as illustrated in the following figure.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="cyclic_usage_details.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Identifying suspicious usages" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></li></ol><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><br />As you can see in the example map below, <em>MD_customization_for_SysML</em> uses itself by using <em>ISO-80000</em> which uses <em>QUDV_SysML 1.4</em> and <em>QUDV_SysML 1.4</em> uses <em>MD_customization_for_SysML</em> thus forming a usage loop.</span></p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="cyclic_usages.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Identifying suspicious usages" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="line-height: 1.66667;letter-spacing: normal;text-align: center;">An example of a cyclic usage map.</h6><h3 style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Unused resources</h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The unused resources filter allows you to see which resources are not used by other resources stored in the Teamwork Cloud repository. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304002408 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00137: (2026x Refresh1) Importing users and groups

- page_id: `304002408`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/304002408/2026x+Refresh1+Importing+users+and+groups
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T10:47:33.893+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) User Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Admin web applications > (2026x Refresh1) Users application
- labels: ['unrestored-unknown-attachment', 'mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can import external users and user groups from LDAP servers to Teamwork Cloud.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

- To import users or user groups at least one LDAP directory must be added to Teamwork Cloud (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Adding and configuring LDAP server' space='']).
- Because of the LDAP export limit, you can only import 1000 users at once.
- It is not possible to import users or user groups with the names that already exist as internal.

To import LDAP users or user groups

1. In the Users application, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] and select [IMAGE alt='' src=''].
2. Select the LDAP server from which you want to import users and user groups and click OK .
3. In the search bar of the LDAP server pane, enter the name of the user or user group you want to import. If you want to get the list of all users stored in the selected LDAP server, type the asterisk sign (*) in the search bar.
4. Select the check-boxes next to the users and user groups you want to import.
5. Optionally click the Set roles button and assign specific roles to the users and user groups to be imported. For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Adding roles' space=''] .
6. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='import.jpg'] to start the import.

After completing the above steps, you will get a notification that the import process has started. If you import a large number of users at once, it may take a while before the import is finished.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Importing users and user groups in the Users application.

When imported users are new, external users are created and the selected roles are assigned to them (see step 5). If you import users that have been imported before, the existing user information is synchronized during the import process but the role assignments stay the same. For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Synchronizing users and user groups' space=''].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

users or user groups</p><hr /><ol><li><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the Users application, click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:width="25"><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_users.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Importing users and groups" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> and select <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="18"><ri:attachment ri:filename="import_users_and_groups.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Importing users and groups" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.</span></p></li><li>Select the LDAP server from which you want to import users and user groups and click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li><p>In the search bar of the LDAP server pane, enter the name of the user or user group you want to import.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="11e0cbf0-1d36-4c78-a0eb-da879dc20c47"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>If you want to get the list of all users stored in the selected LDAP server, type the asterisk sign (*) in the search bar.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li>Select the check-boxes next to the users and user groups you want to import.</li><li>Optionally click the <strong>Set roles</strong> button and assign specific roles to the users and user groups to be imported. For more information, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Adding roles" /></ac:link>.</li><li>Click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:width="25"><ri:attachment ri:filename="import.jpg"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Importing users and groups" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> to start the import.</li></ol><p><br />After completing the above steps, you will get a notification that the import process has started. If you import a large number of users at once, it may take a while before the import is finished.</p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:height="400"><ri:attachment ri:filename="importing_users_and_groups.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Importing users and groups" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Importing users and user groups in the Users application.</h6><p>When imported users are new, external users are created and the selected roles are assigned to them (see step 5). If you import users that have been imported before, the existing user information is synchronized during the import process but the role assignments stay the same. For more information, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Synchronizing users and user groups" /></ac:link>.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304002067 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00138: (2026x Refresh1) Importing users and groups from LDAP

- page_id: `304002067`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/304002067/2026x+Refresh1+Importing+users+and+groups+from+LDAP
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T10:47:17.066+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) User Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Admin web applications > (2026x Refresh1) Settings application > (2026x Refresh1) Managing LDAP servers
- labels: ['importing-ldap-users', 'importing-ldap-group', 'synchronizing-users', 'synchronizing-groups', 'copy-assignment-in-ldap-form', 'copy-assignment-while-importing', 'assign-role-while-importing', 'mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can import users and groups after all LDAP server configuration is completed. Teamwork Cloud importing also works as a user synchronization mechanism. Existing users will only be synchronized and new users in the Teamwork Cloud Admin will be imported. You can see user data in the **Users application**before choosing to import users. This importing and synchronization mechanism applies to groups as well. Data, including full name, phone, email, and department, will be synchronized.

##### Importing and synchronizing users and groups

To import/synchronize users and groups

1. On the selected LDAP server card click the **Import** button. The **Import LDAP user/group** pane opens.
2. Query all users/groups or specific users/groups and select them.
3. Click the [ATTACHMENT filename='import_ldap_users.jpg'] button. New users/groups will be imported or existing users/groups will be synchronized. The existing user information will be updated but the role assignment stays the same.

##### Managing roles

You can assign roles in the same pane, while selecting users and groups to be imported.

###### Assigning roles

To assign a role from one user/group to another

1. On the Import LDAP user/group pane, next to Role assignments , click Set Roles .
2. In the Set roles pane, type a role name in the search bar.
3. Select a role. If it is a custom role, specify scope.
4. Click the Save button to save the changes.

###### Copying assignments

To copy assignment(s) from one user/group to another

1. On the Import LDAP user/group pane, next to Role assignments , click Set Roles .
2. In the Set roles pane, type a username whose assignments you want to copy in the search bar.
3. Select a user/group. The assignments associated with the selected user/group will appear.
4. Click the Save button to save the changes. The selected assignments will be assigned to the target user/group.

##### Troubleshooting

If you cannot synchronize users and/or groups, check the **server.log** file for a message warning about illegal characters. For example:

```text

```

If you see a similar message, allow the use of the character in the **application.conf** file:

```text

```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

Existing users will only be synchronized and new users in the Teamwork Cloud Admin will be imported. You can see user data in the <strong>Users application </strong>before choosing to import users. This importing and synchronization mechanism applies to groups as well. Data, including full name, phone, email, and department, will be synchronized. </p><h3>Importing and synchronizing users and groups</h3><p>To import/synchronize users and groups</p><hr /><ol><li><p>On the selected LDAP server card click the <strong>Import</strong> button. The <strong>Import LDAP user/group</strong> pane opens.</p></li><li>Query all users/groups or specific users/groups and select them. </li><li>Click the <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:width="27"><ri:attachment ri:filename="import_ldap_users.jpg"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Importing users and groups from LDAP" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> button. New users/groups will be imported or existing users/groups will be synchronized. The existing user information will be updated but the role assignment stays the same.</li></ol><h3>Managing roles</h3><p>You can assign roles in the same pane, while selecting users and groups to be imported.</p><h4>Assigning roles</h4><p>To assign a role <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">from one user/group to another</span></p><hr /><ol><li>On the <strong>Import LDAP user/group</strong> pane, next to <strong>Role assignments</strong>, click <strong>Set Roles</strong>. </li><li>In the <strong>Set roles</strong> pane, type a role name in the search bar.</li><li>Select a role. If it is a custom role, specify scope.</li><li>Click the <strong>Save</strong> button to save the changes. </li></ol><h4>Copying assignments</h4><p>To <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">copy assignment(s) from one user/group to another</span></p><hr /><ol><li>On the <strong>Import LDAP user/group</strong> pane, next to <strong>Role assignments</strong>, click <strong>Set Roles</strong>. </li><li>In the <strong>Set roles</strong> pane, type a username whose assignments you want to copy in the search bar.</li><li>Select a user/group. The assignments associated with the selected user/group will appear.</li><li>Click the <strong>Save</strong> button to save the changes. The selected assignments will be assigned to the target user/group.</li></ol><h3>Troubleshooting</h3><p>If you cannot synchronize users and/or groups, check the <strong>server.log</strong> file for a message warning about illegal characters. For example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8202c58b-505a-47d4-9c76-eb2114437c58"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[INFO 2023-10-17 01:00:01.111Z CN=Name, OU=Users Has illegal character [\\]. Cannot be imported]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>If you see a similar message, allow the use of the character in the <span><strong>application.conf</strong> file:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2b268c43-749e-47fc-bcec-5359bd1b4603"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[esi.ldap.allowBackSlashChar = true]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304002583 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00139: (2026x Refresh1) Inserting content from another document

- page_id: `304002583`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/304002583/2026x+Refresh1+Inserting+content+from+another+document
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T10:47:52.542+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) User Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud > (2026x Refresh1) Editing and contextualizing > (2026x Refresh1) Contextualizing Cameo Collaborator documents
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can include content, such as diagrams and tables, from one document into another. Keep the included content up-to-date by selecting to display its latest version or include thecurrentcontent version to make sure it will stay the same.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

To add media to a Cameo Collaborator document:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Publishing documents' space=''] .
- You need the Read Resources permission or Resource Reviewer role for the document.
- You need the Read Resources and Edit Resources permission or Resource Contributor role for the project with read-write permissions for the project branch.

To insert content from another Cameo Collaborator document

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Opening a resource' space=''].
2. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] on the bottom right corner of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Content pane' space=''].
3. Click the **Insert from document** icon in the location of an open document section where you want to include content. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. In the open wizard, select the document from which you want to insert the content.
5. In the next step, navigate to and select the document section from which you want to insert the content.
6. Select the element of the section you want to insert. You can only insert tables and diagrams.
7. Select one of the following insert settings:
  - Display latest version - automatically updates the inserted content to the latest version when it becomes available.
  - Display current version (at the time when inserting) - always displays the content version that was available at the time of insertion. [ATTACHMENT filename='insert_settings.png']
8. Click the Add button.
9. Repeat steps 3 to 8 to insert more content or click [ATTACHMENT filename='cancel_editing.png'] on the bottom right corner of the content pane to exit the editing mode.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304001708 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00141: (2026x Refresh1) Installation on Linux

- page_id: `304001708`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/304001708/2026x+Refresh1+Installation+on+Linux
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T10:46:43.962+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Deployment Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Installation
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The following sections describe the full deployment of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud on a single Linux server. The deployment includes installation of third-party components: Apache Cassandra database and FlexNet license server. A set of installation scripts are provided to streamline the installation process. This type of all-in-one deployment should be used for testing and evaluation. For a performant system, we recommend distributing components and services on multiple servers.

The following Linux packages are required for each service. All these packages are automatically pulled from online repositories when using the included installation scripts. For offline installation, refer to the specific installation sections for more information on accessing these packages. Also refer to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) System requirements' space=''] section for version information.

For a complete list of service files, please see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Web Application Platform services' space=''].

| Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud |
| --- |
| Open JDK Java (see )Linux font package: dejavu-serif-fonts (optional) |
| Apache Cassandra |
| Open JDK Java (see )Cassandra and Cassandra Tools |
| FlexNet 11.19 |
| Existing Java installation (see )Linux utility package: unzipFlexNet 64-bit Installer and UtilitiesCameo Daemon for FlexNet |

**Prior to installation:**

- Make sure that your hardware and software meet the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) System requirements' space=''].
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Preparing the operating system' space=''] the Linux system for Cassandra database deployment.
- [Download](https://docs.nomagic.com/MCS/latest/magic-collaboration-studio/downloadable-scripts-283246728.html)and extract the installation script package *TWC_MCS_install_scripts_<version>.zip*
  - Extract the package in the same location as the installer .bin file
  - Add execute permission to all script files

The provided installation scripts are designed to streamline the installation process and automate system configuration. These scripts require remote access to official online repositories by default. Offline installation is possible if prerequisites are available locally on the system. For more information, refer to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Product FAQ' space=''].

To install and configure a working instance of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud, follow the guides below:

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Installing Apache Cassandra' space=''] .
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Installing the FlexNet server' space=''] .
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Installing services' space=''] .

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Product installation and configuration on a single Linux server.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304001487 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00142: (2026x Refresh1) Installation on Windows

- page_id: `304001487`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/304001487/2026x+Refresh1+Installation+on+Windows
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T10:46:28.034+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Deployment Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Installation
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Teamwork Cloud installation and configuration on a single Windows server.

This section provides instructions on how to install Cassandra and Magic Collaboration Studio /Teamwork Cloud on Windows. During the installation, the installer asks you to provide the required configuration values and creates a Windows service for you.

After completing the installation, make sure you grant access through the firewall to the required ports, as shown in the diagram above.

Once the installation is complete, you need to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Applying the license' space=''] to start working with Teamwork Cloud in MagicDraw and using Teamwork Cloud Admin.

To install and set up Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudon Windows, complete the following steps:

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='FlexNet license server installation and licensing' space='IL'] .
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Installing Cassandra on Windows' space=''] .
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Installing services on Windows' space=''] .
4. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Starting services on Windows' space=''] .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target" style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="teamwork_cloud_single_node_deployment.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Installation on Windows" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Teamwork Cloud installation and configuration on a single Windows server.</h6><p>This section provides instructions on how to install Cassandra and <span>Magic Collaboration Studio / </span>Teamwork Cloud on Windows. During the installation, the installer asks you to provide the required configuration values and creates a Windows service for you.</p><p>After completing the installation, make sure you grant access through the firewall to the required ports, as shown in the diagram above.</p><p>Once the installation is complete, you need to <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Applying the license" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[apply the Teamwork Cloud license]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> to start working with Teamwork Cloud in MagicDraw and using Teamwork Cloud Admin.</p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To install and set up <span>Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>on Windows, complete the following steps:</span></p><ol><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="IL" ri:content-title="FlexNet license server installation and licensing" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Install FlexNet license server]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Installing Cassandra on Windows" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Install Cassandra on Windows]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Installing services on Windows" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Install the product on Windows]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Starting services on Windows" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Start Teamwork Cloud on Windows]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li></ol>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304001863 space=MCS version=3 -->
## PAGE 00143: (2026x Refresh1) Installing Apache Cassandra

- page_id: `304001863`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/304001863/2026x+Refresh1+Installing+Apache+Cassandra
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2026-05-27T08:55:08.920+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Deployment Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Installation > (2026x Refresh1) Installation on Linux
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Clouduses Apache Cassandra, an open-source NoSQL distributed database. Before installing Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud, please follow the steps below to install Apache Cassandra.

##### Prerequisites

- OpenJDK Java (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Third-Party Component Compatibility' space=''] for version information)
- Apache Cassandra RPMs
  - cassandra- <version_number> .noarch.rpm
  - cassandra-tools- <version_number> .noarch.rpm

##### Installing with script

The script downloads and installs the necessary packages, Cassandra, and the Cassandra tools from the Apache Software Foundation repository, and creates the necessary firewall rules to allow proper operation both for a single node or a cluster installation. The script will also install the required Java version and set it as the default system Java.

The script can also be used for offline installation, download the prerequisite RPM packages and place them in the same location as the installation script. Manually install required Java version and set it as the system default Java.

To install Apache Cassandra

1. Install Apache Cassandra by executing the*install_cassandra<version_number>_ol_rhel.sh* installation script. Exampletrue_ol_rhel.sh]]>
2. Start Apache Cassandra by executing the following command: bashDJangotrue
3. Check if Apache Cassandra is running by executing the following command: bashDJangotrue If Apache Cassandra is running, you should receive the output displayed below. If the service is fully operational, the first 2 characters of the last line are "UN", indicating that the node status isUp, and its state isNormal. bash Wait for a few minutes until Cassandra starts for the first time before checking if it is running. If Cassandra has not started yet, you will get the error: "No nodes present in the cluster. Has this node finished starting up?" This means that you need to give Cassandra more time to start.
4. If Apache Cassandra is not running or if you used installation options other than the one described in this chapter, optionally .

##### Developing a backup strategy

Before deploying Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudand Apache Cassandra in a production environment, it is imperative to have a fully implemented backup strategy. The Cassandra database stores all project and user data associated with Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud. Review the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Backup and restore data procedures' space=''] document. Ensure that you test the entire backup and restore process before your deployment goes live to users.

During the backup process, user access to Cassandra should be suspended. Refer to the [Cassandra backup documentation](https://cassandra.apache.org/doc/4.0/cassandra/operating/backups.html) for more information.

Improper backup procedure can lead to total data loss! For example, taking an image snapshot of the storage system while Cassandra is actively accepting read and write requests will result in unrecoverable data.

##### Configuring Apache Cassandra for Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud

If you used other installation options and not the provided script or if Apache Cassandra does not start, configure it as described below.

install_cassandra<version_number>_<os_version>.sh

To configure Apache Cassandra

1. Locate the *cassandra.yaml* file (default: /etc/cassandra/conf/cassandra.yaml) and open for editing.
2. Find the following parameters related to the Cassandra node IP address and communication settings, and change them as shown below: bashExample
  - seeds - a comma-delimited list containing all of the seeds in the Cassandra cluster. Since our cluster consists of a single node, it contains only one entry - our IP address.
  - listen_address - the IP address that Cassandra uses to listen for connections.
  - broadcast_rpc_address - the IP address used to broadcast to other Cassandra nodes in the cluster. This parameter may be commented. In such a case, remove the "#" and ensure there are no leading spaces.
  - rpc_address - when set to 0.0.0.0, Cassandra listens to rpc requests on all interfaces.
3. Find the following parameters that control thresholds to ensure that the data being sent is processed properly, and change them as shown below: bashExample
4. To ensure that the default commit log size is 8GB (recommended), uncomment the**commitlog_total_space** parameter as shown below. bashExample Ensure that the partition where the commit log is installed has enough space to accommodate a commit log of 8GB.
5. To point the data to the appropriate locations, find the following parameters and change them as shown below: bashExample
6. Start Apache Cassandra by executing the following command: bashDJangotrue
7. Check if Apache Cassandra is running by executing the following command: bashDJangotrue If Apache Cassandra is running, you should receive the output displayed below. If the service is fully operational, the first 2 characters of the last line are "UN", indicating that the node status isUp, and its state isNormal. bashExample

##### Configuring Cassandra memory usage

If you did not use the installation script or want to increase the RAM usage by Cassandra, make the following changes. Otherwise, these configuration changes are set automatically by the Cassandra installation script.

Set the maximum direct memory size to ensure your off-heap structures (like memtables and caches) have a dedicated, stable pool of memory and prevent the node from crashing due to memory limits.

To configure the maximum direct memory size, you must explicitly set the standard JVM argument -XX:MaxDirectMemorySize, in the appropriate JVM options file jvmXX-server.options.

- Default file location: /etc/cassandra/conf/jvmXX-server.options
- You may need to add the -XX:MaxDirectMemorySize line if it is missing, or edit the existing one.
- Replace jvmXX with your installed Java version (read requirements for the deployed version).

**Edit jvmXX-server.options and add the line:**

```text

```

To configure the Java Heap space for Cassandra, please see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Configuring Java heap space' space=''].

##### Configuring Cassandra CPU usage

- In the jvmXX-server.options file, uncomment the following lines and set the values to the physical CPU core count (the values of both parameters should be the same):

```text

```

##### Additional configuration

- Synchronize CPU clocks on all Cassandra cluster nodes. Otherwise, you may encounter issues when creating an empty Cassandra cluster.
- When using cqlsh , the Python version required for cqlsh to work with Cassandra 5.0 is Python 3.8 to 3.11.
- In the logback.xml file, comment the "<appender-ref ref="ASYNCDEBUGLOG" />" line. This will increase Cassandra's performance by disabling the debug log.

##### Configuring Linux environment for Cassandra performance

If you install Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudusing the *install_twc_mcs_centos_rhel.sh* script, Cassandra performance is tuned automatically. However, if you plan to use other installation options or if you need to set other parameters after running the script, you can do it manually as described in this section.

To improve Apache Cassandra performance

1. Locate the *sysctl.conf* file (default: /etc/sysctl.conf) and open it for editing.
2. To configure the TCP settings, add the following tuning parameters to the file: bashExample
3. To apply the setting without rebooting, execute the following command: bashDJangotrue

For more information about tuning Linux, see [DSE 6.9 Administrator Guide](https://docs.datastax.com/en/dse/6.9/managing/configure/recommended-settings.html).

###### Using jemalloc memory allocator

The jemalloc memory allocator package can potentially improve Cassandra performance. Our installation script does not install jemalloc. The easiest way to install this optional package is to first install the epel-release package. You can then pull the latest jemalloc release from the EPEL repository. An older version jemalloc is available for direct download and installation.

- EPEL package (optional, for pulling jemalloc package)

- jemalloc package (optional for performance)

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

Ensure that you test the entire backup and restore process before your deployment goes live to users.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d332610a-ceff-4e66-85c4-f5b61ab0b956"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>During the backup process, user access to Cassandra should be suspended. Refer to the <a href="https://cassandra.apache.org/doc/4.0/cassandra/operating/backups.html">Cassandra backup documentation</a> for more information.</span><span> </span></p><p>Improper backup procedure can lead to total data loss! For example, taking an image snapshot of the storage system while Cassandra is actively accepting read and write requests will result in unrecoverable data.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Configuring Apache Cassandra for <span>Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud</span></h3><p>If you used other installation options and not the provided script or if Apache Cassandra does not start, configure it as described below.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="54cf37b4-fecc-428a-b542-171d628f71bb"><ac:rich-text-body>Before starting, note that you do not need to configure Apache Cassandra if you installed it using the installation script we provided (<em>install_cassandra&lt;version_number&gt;_&lt;os_version&gt;.sh</em>). It should start without any additional configuration.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To configure Apache Cassandra</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="145e460d-f0ca-4809-9036-628c1937aa5a"><p class="auto-cursor-target">Locate the <em>cassandra.yaml</em> file (default: /etc/cassandra/conf/cassandra.yaml) and open for editing.</p></li><li data-uuid="a4c23854-f940-45b9-9332-ccaa195b0535"><p class="auto-cursor-target">Find the following parameters related to the Cassandra node IP address and communication settings, and change them as shown below:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1b6a9752-4694-4d9c-ab86-1b951b51e430"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[seeds: "192.168.130.10"
listen_address: 192.168.130.10
broadcast_rpc_address: 192.168.130.10
rpc_address: 0.0.0.0]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ul><li><strong>seeds</strong> - <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">a comma-delimited list containing all of the seeds in the Cassandra cluster. Since our cluster consists of a single node, it contains only one entry - our IP address.</span></li><li><strong>listen_address</strong> - <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">the IP address that Cassandra uses to listen for connections.</span></li><li><strong>broadcast_rpc_address</strong> - <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">the IP address used to broadcast to other Cassandra nodes in the cluster. This parameter may be commented. In such a case, remove the &quot;#&quot; and ensure there are no leading spaces.</span></li><li><strong>rpc_address</strong> - when set <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">to </span>0.0.0.0, Cassandra listens <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">to rpc requests on all interfaces.</span></li></ul></li><li data-uuid="14debb96-69b6-4098-bcc1-e6287cd10830"><p class="auto-cursor-target">Find the following parameters that <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">control thresholds to ensure that the data being sent is processed properly, and change them as shown below:<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e4cb4630-dcd0-428b-a2e8-b52de1f74a28"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[commitlog_segment_size: 192MiB
read_request_timeout: 1800000ms
range_request_timeout: 1800000ms
write_request_timeout: 1800000ms
cas_contention_timeout: 1000ms
truncate_request_timeout: 1800000ms
request_timeout: 1800000ms
batch_size_warn_threshold: 3000KiB
batch_size_fail_threshold: 5000KiB]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li data-uuid="d11205c2-e6d9-4726-8f6f-8a95598e92c8"><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To ensure that the default commit log size is 8GB (recommended), uncomment the </span><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="78cf8988-7eb0-4f64-bdff-00d0ebcf54ab">commitlog_total_space</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong> parameter as <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">shown below.<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d71783fa-d7d3-4d31-9f6c-2b987cbe5147"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[commitlog_total_space: 8192MiB]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cff29604-57d5-48a5-8e32-b1435084a97c"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Ensure that the partition where the commit log is installed has enough space to accommodate a commit log of 8GB.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li data-uuid="7e3df937-bcb9-41f9-9e57-d77fe04915bc"><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To point the data to the appropriate locations, find the following parameters and change them as shown below:<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5657b9ba-2980-4ecc-a772-a8c5642c99c3"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[data_file_directories:
- /data/data
commitlog_directory: /logs/commitlog
hints_directory: /data/hints
saved_caches_directory: /data/saved_caches]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li data-uuid="88afb16f-0b86-460d-b36e-106ea67fe937"><p class="auto-cursor-target">Start Apache Cassandra by executing the following command:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="489be1f4-0a8b-4c6e-b865-bed3f911251a"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[sudo systemctl start cassandra]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li data-uuid="645ff482-8424-4bd0-8a4a-baca30a0c83d"><p class="auto-cursor-target">Check if Apache Cassandra is running by executing the following command:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="592c72ee-1452-4673-b3d6-1db92d1e6336"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[nodetool status]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">If Apache Cassandra is running, you should receive the output displayed below. <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">If the service is fully operational, the first 2 characters of the last line are &quot;</span>UN<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">&quot;, indicating that the node status is </span>U<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">p, and its state is </span>N<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">ormal.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dec272aa-e4fa-47a2-80ca-5fb891e9ae13"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Datacenter: datacenter1
=======================
Status=Up/Down
|/ State=Normal/Leaving/Joining/Moving
